interview_style.description: SpaceX systems thinking and first-principles style. Fast-paced, direct, engineering-focused. Values people who can build from scratch and reason about failure modes.
interview_style.tone: Direct, no-nonsense, intellectually intense. Expects you to think on your feet. Comfortable with silence while you think. Will challenge assumptions aggressively but respectfully.
interview_style.pacing: Fast. Moves quickly through topics. If you nail it, they advance to harder questions rapidly. If you stumble, they may rephrase once but won't hand-hold. Typical: 45-60 minutes.
interview_style.interviewer_persona: Senior engineer who builds things. Not a professional interviewer. Cares about whether you can actually do the work, not whether you interview well. May sketch diagrams, ask you to whiteboard. Excited by novel thinking.

behavioral_emphasis.description: SpaceX cares less about behavioral frameworks and more about demonstrated intensity, resourcefulness, and ability to work under extreme constraints.
focus_areas: How do you handle impossible timelines?
Tell me about something you built from nothing.
Describe a time you threw away an approach and started over.
How do you make decisions when the standard playbook doesn't apply?
What's the hardest technical problem you've solved?

technical_emphasis.description: Heavily technical. SpaceX security interviews focus on systems thinking, failure mode analysis, and the ability to reason from first principles about security architecture.
focus_areas: First principles: 'If you were building this from scratch, how would you do it?'
Failure modes: 'What breaks first? What's the blast radius?'
Scale thinking: 'How does this work for a global satellite network?'
Resource constraints: 'You have 1 engineer and 2 weeks.'
Systems integration: 'How does this security control interact with the rest of the stack?'
Challenge standard answers: 'Everyone says that. Why is everyone wrong?'

follow_up_patterns: You have half the time and half the resources. What changes?
Everyone says that. What would YOU actually do?
If you were building this from scratch, no legacy constraints, what would it look like?
What's the simplest version of this that still works?
What would Elon challenge about this approach?
How do you know this is the right approach and not just the standard one?
